title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
section: Heat transfer is one of the most practically useful courses in mechanical engineering
text:
Heat transfer is one of the most practically useful courses in mechanical engineering
By the end of this semester, you’ll be able to work through problems like:

A GPU produces 800 W of waste heat and needs to stay below 80°C. If we air-cool it, how fast must the fan spin? If we water-cool it, how high must the flow rate be?
The Trans-Alaska Pipeline carries crude oil across 800 miles of arctic tundra. We must heat the oil to keep the viscosity low, thus reducing the pumping costs, but that extra heat might melt the permafrost, which could destabilize to pipeline supports. How should we design the insulation?
A satellite has no atmosphere to convect heat away. How do we keep the electronics from cooking?

These are the kind of analyses that show up in real engineering work. After taking this class, you will be equipped to do them.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
This page is the course “hub”
Use this website as your primary course resource. Each chapter (Intro, Week 1, Week 2, etc. in the left toolbar) contains the lecture notes, derivations, interactive examples, and links to the associated videos. We will use Canvas for submitting work and checking your grades, but the course content lives on this page.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
What makes this course distinctive
It’s compressed. Eight weeks to cover a full semester of material. The course structure is designed to make that manageable: videos are tightly edited (no long pauses or repeated explanations), derivations live on this website so you can read at your own pace, and the aspects of the course feed-into each other.
It’s asynchronous. You consume the content on your own schedule, though there are weekly deadlines. This requires self-discipline, but the flip side is that you control your own time.
The exams are oral. Rather than pencil-and-paper exams, you’ll have two oral exams: a midterm and a final. These are conversations about engineering design — you will not be asked to grind through algebra under pressure. Students who have done the work consistently might find these conversations genuinely enjoyable.
You’ll talk through problems with peers. Small-group Zoom sessions where you and a few colleagues work through a problem together (over about 30 minutes). These are some of the most authentic engineering experiences you will have during your engineering education.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
How the assessments connect
The course is designed as a connected system:

Tier 1 problems build the fluency you need to write the technical reports
Technical reports create the documents you’ll discuss in the oral exams
Peer discussions give you practice reasoning out loud before those exams
NotebookLM helps you build a searchable knowledge base you can bring to oral exams
Midterm oral is a calibration point — you find out where you stand while there’s still time to improve
Capstone + final oral is where everything comes together

Each component sets you up for the next. See Assessment Overview & Philosophy for the full picture.

title: Assessment Overview & Philosophy
text:
Grade Breakdown
What do engineers actually do? They write technical documents, present their reasoning to colleagues, defend design choices under questioning, and collaborate to solve problems. These are skills, and this course is organized around practicing these skills.
NotebookLM is a modern tool for organizing and recalling your notes. It rewards good note taking and let’s you access your “external brain” via context-filled prompts.
Tier 1 problems build intuition and basic ability with the tools of heat transfer. These won’t require the full detail of a technical report but will get you practice with the content.
Technical reports train you to effectively communicate your work in written form. State your assumptions and justify them, your colleagues who read your documentation in five months will appreciate it!
Peer discussions exist because engineering happens in teams. Explaining your reasoning and assessing your colleagues’ reasoning are extremely important skills.
Oral exams are the most direct way to demonstrate understanding. They are also, for most students, more interesting than a written exam, and indeed are probably more practically relevant than a written exam!

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Overview
Tier 1 problems will get you some practice with the tools of heat transfer analysis and will make up the bulk of your homework. They vary in type – some are conceptual (“why is there a negative sign in Fourier’s law?”), some are quantitative (“find the heat flux through this composite wall”), and some are analytical (“derive an expression for the maximum temperature”). They don’t require a full technical report, but a neat solution with a clearly stated answer and with a bit of narration (“the conduction equation is…”, “melting in about 30 minutes indeed seems realistic”) is necessary.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Why These Problems Exist
The best way to learn a topic is to struggle. You cannot read someone else’s solution to learn a topic. (And LLMs like ChatGPT, fall into the category of “someone else”.) When reading a solution, you are not faced with uncertainty, you don’t have to think about how the information fits together, you do not have to make choices about what path to take, you don’t have to be careful with the units, and you don’t have to verify the reasonableness of your answer – it’s all done for you. You have to do problems yourself to learn the material! Let yourself be confused, that is how to grow! These Tier 1 problems are opportunities to do that.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Submission Format
Each Tier 1 problem follows the same two-stage process as the technical reports:
Stage 1 (Friday, 6:00 AM): Submit a genuine attempt. Legible work, units carried, answer stated. Completion probably earns full credit for this stage. Submitting Stage 1 unlocks the Stage 2 assignment, which includes milestone checkpoints.
Stage 2 (Monday, 6:00 AM): Submit a clean final version. This is lightly graded on correctness, including mathematical analysis, stated assumptions, answers with units, etc.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
section: What a Good Tier 1 Submission Looks Like
text:
What a Good Tier 1 Submission Looks Like

Work is legible and organized
Units appropriately used: \(k=150\) W/m·K (not just \(k=150\))
Answer is clearly stated
One sentence explains the physical meaning of the result: not just “\(Q = 245\) W” but “\(Q = 245\) W, and the positive sign confirms that the heat is flowing out of the object”

That last sentence is often the most valuable part – in this world with omnipresent AI, being able confidently state your answer and justify is becoming increasingly valuable.

title: Technical Reports
text:
Overview
Each week, one of the homework problems will be a technical report. With these we are not just looking for the correct answer, we are looking for clear organization and an explanation of the logic.

title: Technical Reports
text:
The Two-Stage Submission Process
We will have a revision process for the technical reports, the idea being: you submit an initial draft where you work out the physics, and then get some hints with which you can check your work before submitting a final draft. After all, the point of homework is to learn the material! …This approach encourages that attitude and avoids punishing initial misconceptions.

Stage 1: Initial Submission (Friday, 6:00 AM)
Submit a genuine attempt at the problem alongside the Tier 1 problems. This does not need all the details of a technical report – if you don’t fully rationalize your assumptions, that’s fine. Don’t forget those aspects for the final submission, but this first one is about checking your physics.
Grading: Basically on completion. A genuine attempt earns full credit for this stage. Do not try to game this and submit incomplete work – you will have points deducted and will not be able to earnestly answer the reflection question in Stage 2.
As soon as you submit, a new assignment will open on Canvas. It’s description will include some milestone checkpoints that you can use to confirm you have the right approach - e.g., “You should get a Reynolds Number of around 12000”, “The outlet temperature is about 295 K”, “Don’t forget to use \(w/2\) as the characteristic length”. If you have any questions, we can also schedule a 1-on-1 meeting.


As soon as you submit, the milestones will be revealed and the Stage 2 submission portal will open. This means you could submit your Stage 1 and Stage 2 reports on Tuesday, if you desire.


Stage 2: Final Submission (Monday, 6:00 AM)
Submit a complete technical report following the structure below. This submission will be separate from the Tier 1 problems.
Grading: Based on the full rubric described below. The reflection section asks you to describe specifically what you got wrong or nearly got wrong in your Stage 1 submission and how you caught and corrected it – this section requires a genuine Stage 1 attempt.

title: Technical Reports
text:
Report Structure
Every technical report should follow this structure:

Problem Setup & Modeling Choices (~½–1 page) Restate the problem in your own words, then state and justify your modeling assumptions. Each assumption needs a physical reason, not just a label – “\(\dot{q}=0\)” is not justification, but “this plastic will not generate heat, so \(\dot{q}=0\)” is. The goal is to make your model’s scope explicit.
Solution Equations numbered and referenced in text. All numbers have units (not just the solution). Some narration: “Using the resistor model…”.
Results & Sanity Checks (~½ page) Does your answer make sense? Check order of magnitude, limiting behavior, units. Compare to intuition or a simpler case.
Common Pitfall & Correction (~¼ page) What assumption or approach did you initially get wrong or nearly get wrong? How did you detect the error – did units not work out, did a sanity check fail, did a limiting case give a nonsensical result? What did you do to correct it?
This section is one of the most important in the report. Every engineer makes wrong assumptions. The skill is catching them. A strong response to this section is better preparation for the oral exam than a perfect first attempt would have been.
Reflection (~¼ page) What was the hardest part of this problem? What connects to real engineering systems or other course material? What would you want to know more about?

title: Technical Reports
text:
Grading Rubric
Each final submission is graded on 8 points across three dimensions.

Physics Reasoning (4 points)







Score
Description




4
Physical reasoning complete and followable throughout. All assumptions stated and justified. Sanity check attempted with genuine physical interpretation. Numbers have units throughout.


3
Reasoning mostly present. Minor gaps: one assumption unjustified, sanity check is superficial, or isolated unit lapse.


2
Reasoning partially present. Significant gap: major assumption unjustified, sanity check missing, or solution presented without connecting physical logic.


1
Minimal reasoning. Solution may be correct but student cannot be credited with understanding why.


0
No physical reasoning visible.





Common Pitfall & Correction (2 points)







Score
Description




2
Specific error or near-miss identified. Detection method explained clearly (units, sanity check, limiting case, etc.). Correction described. Consistent with Stage 1 submission.


1
Reflection present but vague, generic (“I made a math error”), or partially inconsistent with Stage 1 submission.


0
Section absent, or clearly inconsistent with Stage 1 submission in a way that suggests Stage 1 was not a genuine attempt.





Presentation (2 points)







Score
Description




2
Report follows required structure. Equations numbered and referenced in text. Writing is clear and followable throughout.


1
Mostly organized but with missing elements – unreferenced equations, unclear section, or writing that impedes understanding in places.


0
Missing sections, or writing that consistently impedes understanding.



Total: 8 points per report

title: Technical Reports
text:
Connection to Oral Exams
Your submitted reports become the text interrogated in the oral exams. Expect questions like:

“You wrote X on page 2 – walk me through that reasoning.”
“You assumed Y here – what would change if that assumption didn’t hold?”
“Your sanity check gave Z – is that what you expected, and why?”
“You said in your pitfall section that you initially got the boundary condition wrong – how did you catch that?”

The best preparation for these exam questions is be patient and thoughtful as you write your technical reports.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Overview
There are two oral exams in this course: a midterm oral (~Week 4) and a final oral (~Week 8). Oral exams are uncommon in undergraduate engineering programs. This page explains how they work, what is expected, and how to prepare.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Why Oral Exams
The short answer: they are the most direct window into your actual understanding of the course content. A written solution can be produced without understanding the reasoning behind it, but a conversation cannot. When you are asked to explain why you made a modeling choice, what would change if a parameter shifted, or where your solution would break down – your answer comes from genuine understanding. There is no middle ground.
Moreover, conversations like these are extremely authentic engineering experiences. You present a technical report in a team meeting and a colleague asks a question. You can’t say “hold on, let me ask ChatGPT and then figure out which part of its response is relevant”, you need to actually have ownership of your knowledge to have that human-to-human interaction.
This format is not about trying to make you nervous and make a mistake. Actually, you can make a mistake - staying in the discussion and recognizing your mistake is an extremely compelling demonstration of your wisdom.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Format
Both oral exams follow the same three-part structure:

Part 1: Anchor Question (~4-5 minutes)
A conceptual question, the same for every student, released early in the week of the exam. You will know exactly what this question is before you sit down.
Example: “Walk me through transient conduction in a semi-infinite solid – what is happening physically, what are the key parameters, and what does the solution tell you?”
The anchor question is released in advance to reduce anxiety and reward preparation, not to make the exam easy. A strong answer demonstrates organized conceptual understanding. A weak answer – even on a question you had a week to prepare – tells me something important.


Part 2: Report Defense (~5-7 minutes)
The examiner will draw from one of your submitted technical reports. You will be asked to walk through your reasoning, justify your assumptions, and explain your sanity checks.
Expect questions like:

“You wrote X on page 2 – walk me through that reasoning.”
“You assumed Y here. What would change if that assumption didn’t hold?”
“Your pitfall section says you initially got the boundary condition wrong. How did you catch that?”

The report you defend will be drawn from your submitted work up to that point. For the midterm, this means reports submitted through approximately Week 3, maybe 4. You will not be told in advance which report will be discussed, but obviously will know that the technical report questions are the foundation of this part of the exam.


Part 3: Extension Question (~3-5 minutes)
A “what if” question that modifies a parameter or removes an assumption from the problem you just discussed.
Example: “You found the steady-state temperature distribution assuming \(k=95\) W/m·K. What would change if conductivity was actually 55? Would we need to provide more or less heat from the heater?”
This part of the exam cannot be prepared for by memorizing solutions. It can only be prepared for by understanding the physics well enough to
Total time: approximately 12-17 minutes per student.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Midterm Oral Exam (~Week 4)
Weight: 15% of course grade
The midterm oral is primarily formative – it is a calibration point that tells you where you stand while there is still time to improve. A weak midterm performance is recoverable.
You may bring your NotebookLM notebook to the exam.

Retake Policy
A retake of the midterm oral is available to any student who wants one.
How it works:

The retake score replaces the original score – for better or worse. If you do better, your grade improves. If you do worse, your grade drops. This is intentional: the retake is for students who are genuinely better prepared, not for students who are hoping for an easier question.
The retake uses a different anchor question and draws from a wider report pool. It is designed to be comparable in difficulty, not easier.
To schedule a retake, you must first submit a short written reflection (see below).

Retake reflection (required to schedule):
Submit a paragraph – 3-5 sentences – addressing the following:

What specific concept or question in the exam did you struggle with, and why?
What have you done since the exam to address that gap?
What are you prepared to demonstrate in the retake that you could not demonstrate the first time?

Vague reflections (“I didn’t study enough”) will be returned without scheduling a retake. Specific reflections (“I couldn’t explain why the Biot number assumption broke down in the composite wall problem, so I reworked problems 2 and 4 from the Tier 1 set”) will be scheduled promptly.
The reflection is not graded. It is a commitment device – it filters for students who have actually done additional preparation, and it gives the examiner useful information going into the retake.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Final Oral Exam (~Week 7-8)
Weight: 25% of course grade
Details will follow.

title: Oral Exams
text:
How to Prepare
The most effective preparation is doing the work throughout the course. Students who patiently go through the course videos and readings while taking good notes, engage genuinely with Tier 1 problems, write detailed technical reports, and participate actively in peer discussions consistently will find the oral exams very manageable. Students who do not find them uncomfortable.
Beyond that:

Practice explaining out loud. Writing and speaking are different cognitive acts. Talk through your solutions to a roommate, a study group, or yourself.
Know your reports. You wrote them – you should be able to walk through any part of them without looking at the document.
Prepare your anchor question seriously. You have several days. A well-organized, physically motivated answer to the anchor question sets a strong tone for everything that follows.
Build your NotebookLM. Students who have rich, well-organized notebooks use them fluently in the exam. Students who built them poorly find them unhelpful under pressure.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Logistics
Exams are conducted by the instructor and TAs via Zoom. Scheduling windows will be posted in Canvas approximately two weeks before each exam period. Sign up early – late slots fill last and create unnecessary deadline pressure.
Each exam is approximately 12-17 minutes. Plan for 20 minutes including brief setup and transition time.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Overview
You will participate in 5-6 recorded peer technical discussions over the course of the semester. Each session is conducted via Zoom in groups of 4-6 students, lasts approximately 30-60 minutes (no time constraints), and centers on a novel problem you work through together in real time. Reasoning out loud about a problem you have not seen before is one of the most authentic engineering skills we will develop in this course.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Why Peer Discussions
Writing a technical report gives you time to look things up, revise your reasoning, and present a polished product. A peer discussion gives you none of that. You have to engage with the physics as it unfolds, respond to your peers’ ideas, catch errors in real time, and contribute something that moves the group forward.
This is also excellent preparation for the oral exams. Students who participate actively in peer discussions consistently find the oral exam format less surprising.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Format
Group size: 4-6 students, rotating across sessions so you work with different peers throughout the course
Duration: 30-60 minutes per session
Problem: A novel problem released at the start of the session — one you have not seen before, but drawn from material already covered in the course
Recording: Sessions are recorded via Zoom for assessment purposes. Make sure your name on Zoom is actually your name!
What you are expected to do: Work through the problem together. Explain your reasoning, respond to others’ ideas, ask probing questions, catch errors, and push the group toward a physically sound solution. Your answer will be imperfect, but I’m more interested in hearing your reasoning along the way.
Final 5 minutes: In the last 5 minutes of each session, the group should converge on a collective answer to the core question. This verbal summary will be captured in the transcript and is factored into the assessment. No separate submission is required.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
How You Are Assessed
After each session, an AI language model analyzes the discussion transcript and generates an individual assessment report for each student. You receive your report within 24-48 hours of the session. –>

The Rubric (1-4 scale)








Score
Label
Description




4
Leading
Drove physical reasoning; introduced key concepts; resolved confusion; asked probing questions that advanced the group


3
Contributing
Made at least one substantive physical contribution the group built on


2
Present
Engaged but mostly social or confirmatory — agreed with others, restated ideas, but did not add physical substance


1
Peripheral
Spoke rarely; contributions did not advance physical reasoning



The key principle: substance over quantity. A single probing question that reorients the group’s reasoning scores higher than ten confirmatory statements. Talking frequently is not the same as contributing substantively. The assessment is calibrated to reflect this.


What Your Report Contains
Your individual assessment report includes:

Your key physics contributions, with direct quotes from the transcript
An evaluation of your reasoning quality — whether your contributions reflected genuine understanding or were vague and surface-level
Your discussion role — driver, responder, or peripheral
Your score (1-4)
Any uncertainty flags — places where the transcript was ambiguous about attribution or intent



Transparency
Our first draft of the system prompt is:

You are an engineering education researcher analyzing a transcript of a student technical discussion. Your task is to evaluate each student’s contribution to the group’s physical reasoning — not their social participation, enthusiasm, or quantity of speech.
For each identified speaker, provide: 1. Key physics contributions: Specific moments where this student identified a governing mechanism, invoked a relevant dimensionless number or concept, proposed a simplifying assumption and justified it, or corrected a physical error. Quote the transcript directly. 2. Reasoning quality: Did their contributions reflect genuine physical understanding, or were they plausible-sounding but physically vague? Be specific and critical. Vague or incorrect statements should be noted as such, even if the student expressed them confidently. 3. Discussion role: Did this student drive the direction of the discussion, respond to others’ ideas, ask questions that deepened the analysis, or remain peripheral? Describe the role with specific evidence from the transcript. 4. Participation score: Rate each student 1-4 on this scale: 1 — Peripheral: spoke rarely, contributions did not advance the physical reasoning 2 — Present: engaged with the discussion but contributions were mostly social or confirmatory rather than physically substantive 3 — Contributing: made at least one substantive physical contribution that the group built on 4 — Leading: drove the physical reasoning of the discussion, introduced key concepts, or resolved significant confusion 5. Uncertainty flags: Note any cases where speaker attribution is unclear, where you cannot determine whether a statement reflects genuine understanding or memorized phrasing, or where the transcript is too short or incomplete to support a confident rating.
Important constraints: - Do not reward a student for speaking frequently if their contributions are physically shallow. - Do not penalize a student for speaking less if their contributions were substantive. - A student who asks a genuinely probing question (“but what happens to the Biot number if we change the geometry?”) is contributing more than one who confirms what someone else said. - If a student makes a physically incorrect statement, note it. Do not soften this observation. - Your output should be useful to a skeptical human reviewer. Write as if your ratings will be checked.

There will be an additional paragraph describing the specifics of the problem. This prompt may go through some revisions as we try to make it applicable to 10+ groups, but the spirit will remain the same.


A Note on Incorrect Statements
The AI is instructed to note physically incorrect statements even when delivered confidently. Getting something wrong in a discussion is not penalized heavily — reasoning through an error and correcting it can be a substantive contribution. Confidently stating something incorrect without correction is noted.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
The Contestation Process
If you believe your assessment report is inaccurate, you may contest it.
Window: one week after receiving your report
How to contest: Submit a written response that identifies specifically which part of the assessment you disagree with and why, with reference to the transcript if possible. (“I believe my contribution at approximately 14:30 was substantive because it introduced the Biot number criterion that the group had not yet applied” is a useful contestation. “I think I deserved a higher score” is not.)
What happens: A TA reads the transcript, your report, and your contestation, and makes a final determination. The TA’s decision is final.
Why this process exists: AI assessment is imperfect. Transcripts have attribution errors. Ambiguous statements get miscategorized. The contestation process is a real mechanism, not a formality — if your report is wrong, this is how you correct it.

title: Peer Discussions
section: This Is Also a Research Study
text:
This Is Also a Research Study
The peer discussion assessment system is part of an IRB-approved research study examining how well AI-generated assessments agree with human expert ratings, and how students perceive transparent AI grading.
Participation in the research component is optional and separate from participation in the course activity. You participate in the discussions and receive assessments regardless of whether you consent to research participation. Research participation means your transcript and assessment data may be used in a published study — with all identifying information removed.
You will receive more information about the research study and the consent process.
Whether or not you participate in the research, the assessment process is identical.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Overview
NotebookLM is a free AI tool from Google that retrieves and synthesizes content from documents you upload. Unlike general AI tools like ChatGPT, it can only draw from sources you provide — it cannot generate answers from general knowledge. If the answer is not in your notes, NotebookLM cannot give it to you.
You will use NotebookLM throughout this course to organize and query your lecture notes. The NotebookLM portfolio is worth 10% of your course grade and is submitted at two checkpoints: Week 4 (formative feedback only) and Week 8 (graded).

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Why NotebookLM
Three reasons this tool is part of the course:
First, it encourages better notes. Heat transfer is highly cumulative — material from Week 1 appears in problems in Week 6. A well-organized, searchable notebook makes that cumulative structure manageable rather than overwhelming.
Second, it supports engagement in an asynchronous course. NotebookLM is only as good as the notes you put into it. Weak notes produce a weak notebook. The portfolio submission is partly a check that you are actually watching lectures and engaging with the material.
Third, it models appropriate AI use. There is a meaningful difference between an AI tool that retrieves your own understanding and one that generates answers you do not have. NotebookLM cannot do your thinking for you — it can only help you access thinking you have already done. That is a useful distinction, and one that generalizes beyond this course.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
How NotebookLM Works

Create a free notebook at notebooklm.google.com using your CU Boulder account. (We get a slightly more robust version when we login using CU credentials.)
Upload your lecture notes as sources: PDFs, Google Docs, or text files work well. If you want to talk about preparing your notes using Markdown or LaTeX, let me know and I can prepare a tutorial!
Query your notebook using natural language: “What is the physical significance of the Biot number?” or “Show me my notes on fin efficiency”.
NotebookLM synthesizes relevant content from your uploaded sources and cites which source it drew from.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Setup (Week 1)
There is a course video for Week 1 (~15 minutes) that walks you through NotebookLM.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Portfolio Submission
The portfolio has two components, submitted together at each checkpoint.

Query Log
A curated list of 10-15 queries you found most useful, with brief annotations. For each query:

The query you submitted
A 1-2 sentence annotation explaining why this query was useful — what it helped you retrieve, clarify, or connect

The goal is not a comprehensive log of every query you ran. It is a curated selection that demonstrates you used the tool in a way that supported your learning.
Example of a useful annotated entry:

Query: “What assumptions are required for the lumped capacitance method?”
Annotation: This came up when I was working on Report 2 and couldn’t remember the Biot number criterion. The notebook pulled my notes from the Week 3 lecture and reminded me that Bi < 0.1 is the standard threshold, which I had written down but forgotten. Confirmed my approach was valid.

Example of a less useful annotated entry:

Query: “convection”
Annotation: I looked this up to review convection.

The first example shows genuine engagement. The second does not.


Reflection
For each major course unit, 1-2 paragraphs addressing:

What did you search for that your notebook could not answer — and why not?
How did you resolve the gap? (Rewatched a lecture, worked a problem, asked during office hours, found it in the textbook?)

This section is more valuable than the query log in some ways. The gaps in your notebook are a record of where your notes were thin, where a concept didn’t stick, or where you needed a source your notebook didn’t have. Reflecting on how you resolved those gaps is exactly the kind of metacognitive habit that leads to a greater understanding… of your understanding!

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Grading
This will largely be graded on completion and genuine engagement, not on the sophistication of your notes or of your queries. A notebook built from genuine note-taking with thoughtful queries should earn full marks.
What earns less than full marks: query logs with vague annotations that do not explain what was useful, or reflections that describe no gaps (which suggests either the notebook is not being used or the reflections are not genuine).

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
section: Bringing Your Notebook to Oral Exams
text:
Bringing Your Notebook to Oral Exams
You are strongly encouraged to bring your NotebookLM to both oral exams. You may query it during the exam.
This is an advantage — but only if you built it well. Students with rich, well-organized notebooks find it genuinely useful under pressure. Students who uploaded thin notes find that querying returns thin results at an inconvenient moment.
The oral exam is, among other things, an authenticity check on the portfolio. A student who built a strong notebook uses it fluently. A student who did not built one finds quickly that there is nothing useful to retrieve.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
section: Some ways of thinking about NotebookLM
text:
Some ways of thinking about NotebookLM
Maybe one of these will speak to you?

NotebookLM can be thought of as an “external brain” that contains the details, while you focus on the engineering process. You know there is a correlation for the Nusselt Number for a particular geometry, and instead of digging through your notes to find it, you prompt your notebook and get started writing out the problem while it thinks. In the 15 seconds it takes to retrieve the relevant equation, you have already written out the assumptions and first step of the solution and are ready to plug-in the equation.
Similarly: you can think of yourself as a senior engineer, and NotebookLM is a junior engineer, and you are working on a project together. Don’t blame the junior engineer if there are bogus results, you trained them!
NotebookLM is a way to check that a human colleague (or maybe you in six months) could get up-to-speed on a project based on your notes. If the AI can’t read your work, or is confused by your organization, or makes some incorrect connections, a human would probably do the same. If your notebook is good, a person could use it to pick up a project easily. Future you will thank you.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Some words on note-taking
This aspect of the course is a bit peculiar, particularly for an upper-level course… you get points for taking notes? But you take notes in other classes and don’t get points? We are emphasizing “knowledge management” in this course because it is a skill that is going to become very important to you very soon, with senior design and your career on the horizon. In industry, if your work isn’t documented well, it didn’t happen.
Some students are already taking clean detailed notes - for them, cultivating their notebook will be easier, and they will be less likely to encounter a confused AI… is that an m, or an n? But this isn’t that different from a student who has a stronger background in differential equations: that student will likely have an easier time with the math in this course. Both technical ability and the ability to communicate are being emphasized in this course.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
AI Policy
NotebookLM is indeed an AI tool, though I believe it is an example of “using AI the right way”. There are right ways to use any AI, but the trouble with LLMs, at least in an educational context, is that they are too eager to just do the work for you. “How can I factor a heat generation rate of \(5x(1-x)\) into my conduction equation?” gets an immediate response: “Here is the solution, and here is the maximum temperature, and here is the heat flux out each wall. Is there anything else I can help you with?” That is not serving your development as an engineer. Officially, general AI use is prohibited in this class, though I am not naive enough to think that policing it is a good use of my time.
There are probably wrong ways to use NotebookLM as well. As we saw in the tutorial video, you can push NotebookLM to do a little math for you. I’m not sure how far this can go… I guess you can explore, but make sure you are the one doing your homework.
The best way to learn anything is to struggle through it, grappling with uncertainty, finding and correcting mistakes, checking with intuition along they way. NotebookLM will allow you to still do those things, and thus still learn, while also reducing the tedium of digging through your notes.

title: Week 0: Introduction
text:
From thermodynamics: What are the ways that we can change the energy of a system?



What are the different ways that heat can be transferred?
What are the meanings of thermal conductivity \(k\) and the convective heat transfer coefficient \(h\)? What are their units?
How does the Sun heat up the Earth through the vacuum of space?

title: Week 0: Introduction
section: The Three Modes of Heat Transfer
text:
The Three Modes of Heat Transfer






TipVideo



Start with this video link. Then read this section. By consuming the content in one medium, then reviewing in another, I hope it will solidify your learning!


From thermodynamics, recall that the energy of a system is changed via heat transfer (to or from an object) or work (on or by an object). This class will obviously be focusing on heat transfer. Recognize that the Laws of Thermodynamics say nothing about how to determine the rate of heat transfer! That will be what we will be focusing on: determining the heat transfer rate.
We give the symbol \(\dot{Q}\) to the heat transfer rate, where \(Q\) is the amount of heat (energy) transferred and the dot on top references a time derivative. The units of \(\dot{Q}\) are Watts = Joules/second. We’ve got three “modes” of heat transfer to think about in this course: conduction, convection, and radiation. Here’s a little preview…

Conduction
Conduction is primarily associated with solids. At the microscopic level, energy propagates through lattice vibrations – add energy to one atom and it begins jostling its neighbors, passing the disturbance along like a chain of springs. In metals, there is a second and often dominant mechanism: free electrons move rapidly through the lattice and carry thermal energy with them. This is why metals tend to be far better thermal conductors than non-metals (copper conducts heat roughly 600 times better than glass), and why good electrical conductors are almost always good thermal conductors too.
Both mechanisms depend on material structure: alloying, crystal defects, and temperature all affect how freely energy is transferred. We invented a material property known as the thermal conductivity \(k\) that helps us catalog this behavior. The units of \(k\) are W/m·K.
The basic equation of conductive heat transfer is something like
\[
\dot{Q}_\text{conduction} =A_c k \frac{T_1-T_2}{w}
\]
though that is a bit of a simplification. As will be the case with all heat transfer modes, heat transfer will be increased by area – here we have \(A_c\) as the cross-sectional area. It will also be the case in all modes that heat transfer increases with increasing temperature difference – here maybe between two sides of an object with temperatures \(T_1\) and \(T_2\), separated by a thickness \(w\).


Convection
Convection is associated with fluids – gases or liquids. If a cool evening breeze passes over you on a hot day, that breeze is removing energy from you: convective heat transfer. Intuitively, we expect that the speed of the breeze will effect the heat transfer rate, and that is indeed the case: we will spend a few weeks learning how to calculate \(h\), the convective heat transfer coefficient, as a function of fluid speed. The units of \(h\) are W/m²·K.
The basic equation of convective heat transfer is
\[
\dot{Q}_\text{convection} = h \cdot A_\text{surface} \cdot (T_\text{surface} - T_\infty)
\]
Again we see: an area dependence, here called a surface area \(A_\text{surface}\), and a dependence on temperature difference.


Radiation
Radiative heat transfer occurs any time objects of different temperatures “see” each other – you are probably a bit warmer than your wall right now, and so you are radiating at your wall as you read this. In practice, radiation is often negligible until there is a big temperature difference… the Sun, for example, is about 5800 K, and it is obviously sending a lot of energy our way. The mechanism: it produces photons, which carry energy through the vacuum of space, through Earth’s atmosphere, and to your skin, where the photons are absorbed, and their energy warms you.
The basic equation of radiation is something like
\[
\dot{Q}_\text{radiation} = \varepsilon \sigma F A_\text{surface} \cdot (T_\text{surface}^4 - T_0^4)
\]
Again, we see an area dependence, and something that looks like a dependence on temperature difference, but the temperatures are raised to the fourth power. The Sun’s surface is about 5800 K, while your skin is about 300 K… that temperature term is massive!
Thankfully, there is a term in this equation that is basically “how well does the Sun see you”: \(F\), the view factor. It is a dimensionless number between 0 and 1, and would be quite small because the Sun doesn’t “see you” that well. There is another dimensionless number, the emissivity, \(\varepsilon\), that is a material property that describes how good of an “emitter” you are. And this equation includes a constant, \(\sigma=5.67 \times 10^{-8}\) W/m²·K⁴, the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.

title: Week 0: Introduction
text:
Course outline
To give some idea of the course flow:

Weeks 1-3 will be about conduction: the conduction equation, the thermal resistance model, enhanced heat transfer via fins, and transient conduction.
Weeks 4-6 will be about convection: boundary layers, the significance of turbulence, flow over objects (external flow), flow through objects (internal flow), and heat exchangers.
Weeks 7-8 will be about radiation: the emissivity of real materials, the view factor, and the radiation resistance model.

title: Week 0: Introduction
text:
Some vocabulary
Students should become familiar with these terms. (I will continue to add to the list as the course progresses.)

adiabatic

No heat transfer. Mathematically: at an adiabatic surface, \(dT/dx=0\) (from Fourier’s Law).

insulator

A material with low conductivity. A “perfect insulator” has \(k=0\), but a practical insulator like foam has \(k\approx 0.03\) W/m·K.

parameters and variables

Parameters are things that can adopt different values, but that tend to be kept constant within a problem. Examples are: material properties like density \(\rho\) and viscosity \(\mu\), sizes like length \(L\) and diameter \(D\), and usually the ambient temperature \(T_\infty\). Variables can adopt different values within an analysis, e.g., if we needed to determine the necessary wind speed \(U\) to keep an object below a certain temperature, \(U\) is a variable in that problem.

thermal conductivity

A material property that emerges from the empirical Fourier’s Law. SI units: (Watts)/(meter Kelvin). Note that the Kelvin here is related to a difference in temperature, and a \(\Delta T = 1~\text{K}\) is the same as a \(\Delta T = 1^\circ\text{C}\). That is, no unit transformation is necessary to go between W/m·K and W/m·°C.

thermal diffusivity

A material property defined as \(\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)\), with units m²/s. It describes how quickly a temperature disturbance propagates through a material. A high \(\alpha\) means the material conducts heat readily relative to how much it stores — temperature changes spread fast.

thermal inertia

The product \(\rho c\) (or \(mc\)), with units J/m³·K (or J/K). It describes resistance to temperature change — a large thermal inertia means a lot of energy must be added or removed to shift the temperature. A cast-iron pan has high thermal inertia; a thin aluminum sheet has low thermal inertia.

heat flux

Denoted \(q''\), heat flux is the rate of heat transfer per unit area, with units W/m². In general it is a vector quantity pointing in the direction of heat flow, though we also discuss it in one-dimensional problems. Contrast with \(\dot{Q}\) (total heat transfer rate, in W), which is a scalar quantity that describes the total heat transfer into or out of a system (potentially calculated by integrating the heat flux over the entirety of the surface).

convection coefficient

Also called the heat transfer coefficient, denoted \(h\), with units W/m²·K. It appears in Newton’s Law of Cooling: \(\dot{Q} = h A (T_\text{surface} - T_\infty)\). It is not a material property — it depends on the geometry, the fluid, and the flow conditions.

thermal resistance

Denoted \(R\), with units K/W. By analogy with Ohm’s Law (\(V = IR\)), thermal resistance relates a temperature difference to a heat transfer rate: \(\Delta T =  \dot{Q}R\).

steady state vs. transient

A problem is steady state (or steady) if nothing changes with time: all \(\partial /\partial t = 0\). It is transient if something changes with time.

boundary conditions and initial conditions

Necessary to solve differential equations. Boundary conditions apply to spatial locations, e.g. at \(x=0\), \(T=4\)… that is a boundary condition. Initial conditions apply to points in time, usually \(t=0\). At \(t=0\), \(T=50\)… that is an initial condition.

Biot number

A dimensionless parameter defined as \(\text{Bi} = hL_c/k\), where \(L_c\) is a characteristic length, \(h\) is the convection coefficient, and \(k\) is the solid’s thermal conductivity.

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.1 Think First
This week we are going to focus on conductivity, the dominant mode of heat transfer within solids. The basic picture in your head can be a rod where heat is being applied to one end and removed from the other. That is, heat is “flowing” through the solid.
Starting with Fourier’s Law, we will derive the conductivity equation, a PDE (partial differential equation… but don’t worry). This equation will allow us to solve for temperature distributions as a function of space and time, e.g.: \(T(x,y,z,t)\). We use that to derive an important approach: the “thermal resistance” model for planar, cylindrical, and spherical geometries. This is a productive way of thinking about problems and will be something that we keep coming back to.
For organization reasons, I put some introductory content in an intro page. You should watch/read through that section, then come back here.
Recall
Look Ahead

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
How many boundary conditions are needed to solve a second-order differential equation?
What does the Second Law of Thermodynamics say about the direction of heat flow?



How can we mathematically ingrain the truth that heat flows from hot to cold?
Can we consider time variation of temperature in our material? What if there is a nuclear reaction generating some heat? What if the thermal conductivity varies with temperature or position?
Are we going to need to solve a bunch of differential equations to get the heat transfer through a composite wall (one with multiple materials)?

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.2 Fourier’s Law






TipVideo



Start with this video link, which covers Fourier’s Law and the Conduction Equation. Then read the associated sections.


We have an empirical law that describes heat conduction known as Fourier’s Law. Stated mathematically:


“Empirical laws” are learned/guessed from experimental data… we can’t derive them. In the case of Fourier’s Law, we at least have a bit of the Second Law of Thermodynamics in there: heat flows from hot to cold. But otherwise, we guessed that the heat flux would be proportional to the temperature gradient, and invented a material property called “the thermal conductivity” that is basically the constant of proportionality.
\[
\begin{align}
\text{For one dimension: } &q''=-k \frac{dT}{dx}\\
\text{For three dimensions: } &\mathbf{q}'' &= -k \boldsymbol{\nabla} T.
\end{align}
\]



Symbol
Meaning
Units




\(T\)
always temperature in this class
K or °C


\(q''\)
heat flux
W/m²


\(k\)
thermal conductivity, a material property
W/m·K


\(dT/dx\) or \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}\)
temperature gradient
K/m



The general three-dimensional version of Fourier’s Law involves the gradient \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}\), and so it is a vector equation: the heat flux \(\mathbf{q}''\) is a vector. The thermal conductivity is a material property… for foam, a really good “insulator”, its value is about $k=$0.03 W/m·K. For copper, a really good “heat conductor”, its value is about $k=$400 W/m·K.






Important



The gradient is essentially a temperature difference as we change position, and a temperature difference of 5 K is the same as a temperature difference of 5°C. The thermal conductivity is written in as “per change in temperature”, and so it does not matter if you are dealing with Kelvin or degrees Celsius: \(k=\) 15 W/m·K = 15 W/m·°C. (Per-Kelvin is more standard.)



1.2.1 The significance of the gradient and minus sign
The “heat flux” \(\mathbf{q}''\) is basically the local heat transfer rate per area \(\dot{Q}/A\) (in the continuum limit). It is proportional to the gradient of temperature: \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}T\). Recall that the gradient of a scalar field tells us the direction of greatest increase of that scalar variable. The negative sign in Fourier’s Law tells us that heat flows in the direction of greatest decrease in temperature. That is: heat flows from hot to cold.


1.2.2 The thermal conductivity
The thermal conductivity is a material property. Its value changes even with subtle changes in the material: 6061 aluminum has \(k=150\) W/m·K, while 3003 aluminum has \(k=180\) W/m·K. Moreover, it is a property that is highly dependent on temperature. In this class, we will usually ignore the temperature dependence and will call \(k\) a constant that we evaluate at an average temperature.
There are interesting design opportunities involving materials where \(k\) has high variation (e.g., a conductivity that dramatically increases with temperature), or where \(k\) is direction dependent (e.g. conductivity occurs more readily in the vertical direction owing to the printing of special, orientable molecules), or where composite materials are used (such that different sections of the device have different values of \(k\)).

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.3 The Conduction Equation
Think back to the First Law of Thermodynamics: the Conservation of Energy. We’ll apply it to a section of a solid rod (the dotted rectangle below) and assume that the energy of this section can change due to: heat conduction in, heat conduction out, and heat generation within (e.g. due to chemical or nuclear reaction).


We will be dealing with the Conservation of Energy, written in different ways, in almost every aspect of this course.

  Conduction control volume
  A solid bar shown as two horizontal lines, with a dashed-border control volume in the center. An arrow labeled Q-dot-conduction(x) enters the left face and an arrow labeled Q-dot-conduction(x+Delta x) exits the right face. Q-dot-generation is labeled inside the box, and Delta x labels the width below.
  
    
      
    
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{conduction}(x)\)
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{conduction}(x+\Delta x)\)
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{generation}\)
  
  
    \(\Delta x\)
  

\[
\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{Q}_\text{conduction,in} - \dot{Q}_\text{conduction,out} + \dot{Q}_\text{generation}.
\]
I’ll include the detailed derivation in the collapsed text below. The summary is:

We call on Fourier’s Law for the conduction in at \(x\) and conduction out at \(x+\Delta x\).
We rewrite the heat generation term as a generic \(\dot{Q}_\text{generated} = V \dot{q}\): \(\dot{q}\) is the heat generated per volume at a certain point, and \(V\) is the volume of the section.
We rewrite the energy change term via the thermodynamic model that a change in energy is proportional to the associated change in temperature via the heat capacity \(mc\), where \(m\) is the mass and \(c\) is the specific heat (at constant volume). That is: \(dE = mc dT\).



If you want to be rigorous, we should use the integral of \(\dot{q}\) over the volume, but well just assume this small segment has constant \(\dot{q}\).

The quantity \(mc\) is sometimes called the “thermal inertia”.







NoteClick here to see the details of the derivation.





For the conduction terms, we bring in the 1D version of Fourier’s Law, making sure to include the cross-sectional area \(A_\text{c}\) (remember, the heat flux \(q''\) is “heat transfer per area”). For the \(\dot{Q}_\text{generation}=V\dot{q}\) term, we’ll write the volume as $A_c(x) $ and the mass as \(m=\rho V=\rho A_c(\Delta x)\) (where \(\rho\) is the density). Then we’ve got \[
\rho A_c(\Delta x) c\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \left(-kA_c \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\rvert_x \right) - \left(-kA_c \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\rvert_{(x+\Delta x)}\right)+A_c(\Delta x) \dot{q}(x).
\]
Almost there! Divide through by \(A_c(\Delta x)\) and swap the order of the conduction terms to get the positive one first, leaving
\[
\rho c\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}=\frac{k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}(x+\Delta x)-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}(x)}{\Delta x} + \dot{q}
\]
And then recognize that, if we take the limit as \(\Delta x\rightarrow 0\), the fraction in that equation is the definition of derivative.



We land here, with the one-dimensional conduction equation \[
\boxed{\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \dot{q}(x,t)}
\]
and, thinking back to Calculus 3, it is intuitive that the full three-dimensional conduction equation will be
\[
\boxed{\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \boldsymbol{\nabla} \cdot \left(k \boldsymbol{\nabla}T\right) + \dot{q}}.
\]
Note that the heat generation term can be a function of location and/or time. In general, the conduction equation is a PDE for \(T(x,y,z,t)\) (if the problem is best phrased in Cartesian coordinates) or \(T(r,\theta,z,t)\) (if cylindrical).

1.3.1 A couple quick examples

Example 1.1 (Steady flow, constant conductivity, no heat generation, 1D Cartesian.) The geometry is an infinite slab where \(T(x=0)=T_0\) is known and \(T(x=L)=T_L\) is known. (These are the boundary conditions.) Then the Conduction Equation simplifies to
\[
\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = 0
\]
Integrating twice will leave us with \(T(x) = c x + d\). Applying the boundary conditions:
\[
T(x) = -\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right)x + T_0.
\]






Tip



You can work out the math in detail on your own if you want to check. You can also note that this equation is of the form \(T(x) = c x + d\) (i.e., it solves the differential equation) and it satisfies the two boundary conditions.




Example 1.2 (Unsteady, with constant heat generation of \(\dot{q}_0\), and we’ll assume the object is small such that the spatial variation is negligible.) We’ll assume the object starts at a known temperature \(T(t=0)=T_0\) (an initial condition). Then the Conduction Equation simplifies to
\[
\rho c \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{q}_0
\]
Integrating once will leave us with \(T(t) = \frac{\dot{q}_0}{\rho c}t + d\), and the unknown constant \(d\) comes from the initial condition:
\[
T(t) = \frac{\dot{q}_0}{\rho c}t + T_0
\]






Tip



Check your intuition: if a constant heat generation is happening, will the object change temperature quicker if it has a big specific heat, or a small specific heat?




Example 1.3 (Composite planar wall) This problem will motivate the thermal resistance section. Suppose we have this situation: a “composite wall” made of materials with different thermal conductivities, \(k_1\) and \(k_2\). The left and right boundaries have known temperatures \(T_\text{left}\) and \(T_\text{right}\). It is steady, no heat generation. How should we approach?

  Composite wall diagram
  Two rectangular layers representing a two-material composite wall. The left layer has width L1 and the right layer has width L2, both the same height. T-left labels the left face temperature and T-right labels the right face temperature.
  
  
  
    Tleft
  
  
    Tright
  
  
    \(k_1\)
  
  
    \(k_2\)
  
  
    L1
  
  
    L2
  







Important



The Conduction Equation applies to a single material: there is no way to handle the abrupt change in \(k\) that occurs at \(x=L_1\) using a single equation.


We will need to solve the Conduction Equation twice. Actually, it is the same solution twice (see Example 1.1 above): \(T_1(x) = c_1 x + d_1\) and \(T_2(x) = c_2 x + d_2\). But what are the boundary conditions? \(T_1(0)=T_\text{left}\) is obvious, as is \(T_2(L_1+L_2)=T_\text{right}\). We need two more.
Physical observation #1: If the two rectangles are in perfect contact at \(x=L_1\) (more on this later), they must have the same temperature. We don’t know what that temperature is, but we can say \(T_1(L_1)=T_2(L_1)\).
Physical observation #2: In order for the system to be at steady state, the amount of heat entering material 1 must be the same as the amount of heat leaving material 1 (else material 1 would be increasing in temperature). And the amount of heat entering material 2 must be the same as the amount of heat leaving material 2 (same reason). Assuming \(T_\text{left}>T_\text{right}\), the heat leaving material 1 must be entering material 2. Framing this in terms of Fourier’s Law:
\[
-k_1 \frac{dT_1}{dx}\bigg\rvert_{L_1} = -k_2 \frac{dT_2}{dx}\bigg\rvert_{L_1}.
\]
A little bit messy, but that is our fourth boundary condition!
The algebra is tedious… you can go through it if you like, but I’m going to skip it for now.






TipThink



We just described two linear profiles, \(T_1(x)\) and \(T_2(x)\). Combined, they are continuous but are not smooth at \(x=L_1\). Intuitively, and look at the Fourier’s Law boundary condition: if \(k_1 > k_2\), is the slope of the temperature profile larger in material 1 or 2?

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.4 Thermal Resistance






TipVideo



Start with this video link, which covers the thermal resistance model.



1.4.1 The Ohm’s Law Metaphor
As engineers, we probably don’t care about the temperature at every location within an object. So solving coupled differential equations to get detailed temperature distributions within composite systems, as we did in Example 1.3, is practically not that useful. Instead, we are going to develop a metaphor with Ohm’s Law of electrical resistance, and use that to connect big design variables that engineers do care about: the total temperature difference and the total heat flow.
Recalling your electrical circuits class, Ohm’s Law is
\[
(\text{voltage}) = (\text{current})(\text{resistance}) \implies V=IR.
\]
We will show in this section that we can create a mathematical metaphor with heat transfer: current is our heat transfer rate \(\dot{Q}\), voltage is a temperature difference \(\Delta T\), and the “thermal resistance” is a function of things like thermal conductivity \(k\), cross-section area \(A_\text{c}\), and material thickness \(L\). That is:
\[
V=IR \implies \Delta T = \dot{Q} R_\text{thermal}
\]
or, rearranging and dropping the “thermal” subscript
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R}.
\]
All the same Ohm’s Law rules apply: resistors in series sum together: \(R_\text{tot} = R_1 + R_2\). Resistors in parallel have the rule
\[
\frac{1}{R_\text{tot}}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}.
\]


1.4.2 Assumptions
For this section we’ll use:

Steady-state: \(\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0\)
Constant conductivity: \(\nabla \cdot (k\nabla T) = k\nabla^2 T\)
One-dimensional: \(\nabla^2 T = \frac{d^2T}{dx^2}\) (Cartesian) or \(=\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right)\) (Cylindrical) or \(=\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right)\) (Spherical)
No heat generation: \(\dot{q} = 0\)



1.4.3 Planar Wall (Infinite)
Recall the solution from Example 1.1:
\[
T(x) = -\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right)x + T_0.
\]
Using Fourier’s Law to compute the heat flux:
\[
q'' = -k\frac{dT}{dx} = k\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right).
\]






Note



If \(T_0>T_L\) we have that \(q''>0\). This is meaningful: heat will flow from left to right, in the positive \(x-\)direction. If \(T_0<T_L\), \(q''<0\), which implies heat flow in the negative \(x-\)direction.


To create the Ohm’s Law metaphor, we multiply both sides by cross-section area \(A_\text{c}\)
\[
q''A_\text{c} = \dot{Q} = kA_\text{c}\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right) = \frac{\Delta T}{L/(kA_\text{c})}.
\]
That is:
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{L/(kA_\text{c})} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{planar}}
\]
and our “thermal resistance” for the planar wall geometry is
\[
R_\text{planar} \equiv L/(kA_\text{c}).
\]


1.4.4 “Composite” Wall
This is where the resistor model becomes really valuable: thinking back to Example 1.3, we know the total temperature drop across the system (\(\Delta T_\text{total} = T_\text{left}-T_\text{right}\)). We could write this as the sum of two temperature drops:
\[
\Delta T_\text{total} = (T_\text{left}-T_\text{interface})+(T_\text{interface}-T_\text{right})=T_\text{left}-T_\text{right}.
\]
It works just like a voltage drop! The total voltage drop across two resistors in series is the sum of the drop across the first resistor and the drop across the second resistor. So, continuing our metaphor, we can write a thermal circuit with two resistors in series:



series circuit


and we can compute the total heat transfer through this wall algebraically – we don’t need to redo the calculus every time:
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{total}} = \frac{T_\text{left}-T_\text{right}}{L_1/(k_1 A_1)+L_2/(k_2 A_2)}.
\]


1.4.5 Expanding the Metaphor
Two concepts that expand the utility of this metaphor:
Convection: we saw in our introduction that
\[
\dot{Q}_\text{convection} = h A_\text{surface} (T_\text{surface}-T_\infty) \implies \dot{Q}_\text{convection} = \frac{\Delta T}{1/(hA_\text{surface})}.
\]
That is,
\[
R_\text{convection} = 1/(h A_\text{surface}).
\]
Contact Resistance
If two rigid materials are joined together, it is unlikely that they achieve perfect contact. Rather, there may be small air gaps owing to machining imperfections, surface roughness, etc. Air is a good insulator (low \(k\)), and so this “contact resistance” will inhibit heat transfer.
We can continue the Ohm’s Law metaphor to describe this as another resistor in series, with \[
\dot{Q}=\frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{contact}}
\]
over the interface. Contact resistance values are tabulated as a function of material pairings, usually given on a “per area” basis:
\[
R_\text{contact}=R''_\text{contact}/A_c.
\]
(The units of \(R''\) are m²⋅K/W, so we divide by the area to get the thermal resistance in K/W.) An example: for a ceramic-ceramic interface, we might have \(R''_\text{contact}=0.001\) m²⋅K/W. Contact resistance also is a function of pressure – pushing surfaces together will deform machining imperfections and surface roughness, decreasing the gap and the contact resistance. So: make sure all your bolts are tight!
Adding contact resistance to the composite wall simply involves adding a third resistor in series.






Note



If we were to sketch the temperature profiles \(T(x)\), the contact resistance would give a discontinuous jump in the temperature.


It is sometimes worthwhile to add thermal paste to the contact locations. This fills the gap with a high thermal conductivity material instead of air, improving heat transfer.

text:

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.6 Radial Systems
Let’s start with some intuition. Suppose we have the following cylindrical-shell geometry

and we will assume \(\theta-\) and \(z-\)symmetry, and that it is steady state. The amount of heat entering the shell must match the amount of heat leaving the shell: \(\dot{Q}_\text{in} = \dot{Q}_\text{out}\). But what can we say about the heat fluxes? Is it that \(q''_\text{in} = q''_\text{out}\), or \(q''_\text{in} > q''_\text{out}\), or \(q''_\text{in} < q''_\text{out}\).






NoteClick here to see the answer





\[
q''(r_\text{in}) \cdot A_\text{in}= q''(r_\text{out}) \cdot A_\text{out}
\]
and \(A_\text{in}<A_\text{out}\), so \(q''_\text{in} > q''_\text{out}\).




1.6.1 Cylindrical
For both cylindrical and spherical coordinates, Fourier’s Law for radial heat flow is written as
\[
q''=-k \frac{dT}{dr}.
\]
But, unlike in the planar case, \(q''\) changes as we proceed through the material. We can still use an Ohm’s Law metaphor though!
We will solve the same problem as in the planar case: steady heat conduction with known temperature at the boundaries: \(T(r_\text{in})=T_\text{in}\) and \(T(r_\text{out})=T_\text{out}\). I’ll add the derivation below, it may be useful to to look through it as this is an example of solving a differential equation in cylindrical coordinates.






NoteClick here to see the derivation





Start from the conduction equation with \(k = \text{constant}\), and we will assume steady heat transfer and no heat generation. Then we have, in cylindrical coordinates
\[
0=\nabla^2 T = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) + \underbrace{\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial \theta^2}}_{\to 0,~\text{symmetry}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial z^2}}_{\to 0,~\text{symmetry}}
\]
that is
\[
0=\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{d T}{d r}\right).
\]
Solving this by integration, we find
\[
T(r)=c_1 \ln r + c_2
\]
and applying the boundary conditions:
\[
T_\text{in} = c_1 \ln r_\text{in} + c_2
\]
\[
T_\text{out} = c_1 \ln r_\text{out} + c_2.
\]
Algebra: \[
T_\text{out} - T_\text{in} = c_1 \ln\left(\frac{r_\text{out}}{r_\text{in}}\right) \rightarrow  c_1 = \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})}
\]
and
\[
c_2 = T_\text{in} - \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \ln r_\text{in}
\]
gets us this:
\[
T(r) = T_\text{in} + \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_\text{in}}\right).
\]
Then the heat flux is
\[
q''(r) = -k\frac{dT}{dr} = -k \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \frac{1}{r}
\]
and the total heat transfer rate is
\[
\dot{Q} = q''(r) \cdot A(r) = k\frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot w
\]



We land here:
\[
\dot{Q} = k \frac{\Delta T}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot 2\pi w
\]
where \(w\) is the cylinder length in the \(z-\)direction. Bringing back the Ohm’s Law metaphor
\[
\dot{Q}= \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{cylindrical}}
\]
and so we see
\[
R_\text{cylindrical}\equiv \frac{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})}{2\pi k w}
\]


1.6.2 Spherical Case
Same process for spherical – steady, angular symmetry, known inner and outer temperatures. The Laplacian operator in spherical (with radial symmetry) is
\[
0 = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right)
\]
Solution process is the same, Fourier’s Law is \(q''=-k(dT/dr)\), and let’s skip to…
\[
\dot{Q} = q''(r) \cdot A(r) = \left(\frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}\right) \cdot \frac{k}{r^2} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \left(\frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}\right) 4\pi k
\]
From which we see
\[
R_\text{sphere} = \frac{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}{4\pi k}.
\]


1.6.3 Applying these equations
We can have composite cylinders/spheres: concentric rings of different materials and different thicknesses. We can add convection on the interior and exterior (remembering that the surface area of a cylinder is \(A_\text{surf}=2\pi R L\) and of a sphere is \(A_\text{surf}=4\pi R^2\)). We can write resistors in series, and if we know the interior and exterior temperatures, we can determine the total amount of heat transfer out of these configurations. Nice.

title: 2  Fins
text:
2.1 Think First
This week we are going to focus on fins, a useful item in our engineering design toolkit. The idea is this: Hot surface! We need to cool it! But the fluid passing over it isn’t creating a high enough \(\dot{Q}\). If our electronics are producing 200 W, but we are only able to remove 100 W, we are going to cook our electronics.
We could increase the flow over the object (bigger fan), change the medium of the flow (water-cooled is better than air-cooled), or put the object in a room with lower temperature (data centers are usually very cold). We are going to focus on another approach: increasing surface area. We will do so by putting “fins” on the object – objects with a high surface area to volume ratio, e.g. numerous 10 mm-long, 2 mm-diameter cylinders. These will wick heat from the object via conduction, and their large surface area will allow greater opportunity to deposit that heat in the fluid via convection.
We’ll examine another version of conservation of energy – this time, 1D only, though we’ll include some convection out (radially) within that description. “The Fin Equation” is an ordinary differential equation for temperature as a function of position, and it will include in it considerations of: the thermal conductivity, the convection coefficient, and the geometry of the fin. In the case of constant cross-section fins, we can solve the Fin Equation analytically.
Recall
Look Ahead
